en.extract:
The red-browed finch (Neochmia temporalis) is an estrildid finch native to the east coast of Australia. This species has established introduced populations near Perth and in the Darling Ranges, and is also feral in French Polynesia. It is commonly found in temperate forest and dry savannah habitats. It may also be found in dry forest and mangrove habitats in tropical regions.
The species is distinguished by the bright red stripe above the eye and bright red rump. The rest of the body is grey, with olive wing coverts and collar. Juveniles do not have red brow marks and lack olive colouration on the collar and wing coverts. The adults are 11–12 cm (4.3–4.7 in) long.


Taxonomy
The red-browed finch was first described by the English ornithologist John Latham in 1801 under the binomial name Fringilla temporalis.
It is one of four species in the genus Neochmia. Alternate names include red-browed firetail, Sydney waxbill and redbill.
The species was once allied to genus Emblema.
There are three noted subspecies: the nominate species N. temporalis temporalis, in most of the east coast, and inland New South Wales and Victoria; N. temporalis minor, which is distinguished by a white breast, in northern New South Wales,  and south-east of Australia, and N. temporalis loftyi in the south west corner of South Australia, although the latter is sometimes not listed as a subspecies, as the differences between it and the type species are relatively minor.


Conservation status
The finch is common in the south east of its range, from Brisbane to Melbourne. Subspecies N. t. minor is common between Cooktown and Townsville. The species is listed as being of least concern on the IUCN Red List. There are no key threatening processes for N. temporalis, although the Pest Animal Control CRC suggests that the introduced nutmeg mannikin (Lonchura punctulata), which currently threatens some native mannikins through increased competition, maybe a minor threat to N. lateralis in northern Queensland.
In regional areas, the species needs adequate shrub density to provide cover and foraging places. The species has been noted to decline or even disappear in areas that have been cleared or grazed, especially in combination with drought.


Biology and ecology
The red-browed finch is highly sociable and is usually seen in small flocks of 10 to 20 individuals. Flocks are sedentary or nomadic in their local area. Flocks prefer semi-open woodland, especially edges of forests, where brushy scrub meets cleared areas, especially near creeks.
The finch makes short, piping high-pitched cheeps. When disturbed, the whole flock will disperse, cheeping, and re-congregate nearby.
N. temporalis is a seed eater, living mostly on grass and sedge seed, but will happily feed on many non-native seeds. Wild birds will even enter large-mesh aviaries in suburban areas to eat seed, given the opportunity.
The red-browed finch builds a large domed nest with a side entrance, woven from grass and small twigs. Nests are usually built 2–3 metres (6.6–9.8 ft) above the ground in dense shrubs. Nesting is communal. Both parents share nest building, incubation of the eggs, and feed the young together. Four to six white eggs are laid per clutch two or three times per year, between October and April. Juveniles are fully independent within 28 days.
Red-browed finches are common aviary birds. The red-browed finch will sometimes hybridise with the star finch, crimson finch, and zebra finch if kept together in captivity.
The species has been introduced to southwest Australia, where it is sometimes confused with the red-eared firetail Stagonopleura oculata.

The '''red-browed finch''' ('''''Neochmia temporalis''''') is an [[estrildid finch]] native to the east coast of [[Australia|Australia.]] This species has established introduced populations near [[Perth]] and in the [[Darling Scarp|Darling Ranges]], and is also feral in [[French Polynesia]]. It is commonly found in temperate forest and dry savannah habitats. It may also be found in dry forest and mangrove habitats in tropical regions.

The species is distinguished by the bright red stripe above the eye and bright red rump. The rest of the body is grey, with olive wing coverts and collar. Juveniles do not have red brow marks and lack olive colouration on the collar and wing coverts. The adults are {{Convert|11-12|cm||abbr=on}} long.<ref name="morcombe">{{Cite book
| edition = Compact
| publisher = Steve Parish Publishing
| last = Morcombe
| first = Michael K.
| title = Field guide to Australian birds
| location = Archerfield, Qld
|isbn=978-1-74021-417-9
| year = 2003
}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The red-browed finch was first described by the English ornithologist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] in 1801 under the [[binomial name]] ''Fringilla temporalis''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Latham | first=Joannis | author-link=John Latham (ornithologist) | year=1801 | title=Supplementum indicis ornithologici sive systematis ornithologiæ | place=London | publisher=Leigh & Sotheby | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/313412#page/54/mode/1up | page=xlviii }}</ref>
It is one of four species in the genus ''[[Neochmia]]''. Alternate names include red-browed firetail, Sydney waxbill and redbill.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Neochmia-temporalis|title=Bird Finder: Red-browed Finch|author=Birds Australia|date=19 February 2007|work=Birds in Backyards|publisher=Australian Museum|access-date=8 October 2011|location=Sydney|archive-date=16 March 2010|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100316034744/http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Neochmia-temporalis}}</ref><ref name="Morcombe1986" />

The species was once allied to genus ''[[Emblema (bird)|Emblema]]''.<ref name="Morcombe1986">{{cite book |last1=Morcombe |first1=Michael |title=The Great Australian Birdfinder |date=1986 |publisher=Lansdowne Press |location=Sydney |isbn=978-0-7018-1962-0 |page=394 }}</ref>
There are three noted subspecies: the nominate species ''N. temporalis temporalis'', in most of the east coast, and inland [[New South Wales]] and [[Victoria (Australia)|Victoria]]; ''N. temporalis minor'', which is distinguished by a white breast, in northern New South Wales,  and south-east of [[Australia]], and ''N. temporalis loftyi'' in the south west corner of [[South Australia]], although the latter is sometimes not listed as a subspecies, as the differences between it and the type species are relatively minor.<ref name="morcombe" />

==Conservation status==
The finch is common in the south east of its range, from [[Brisbane]] to [[Melbourne]]. Subspecies ''N. t. minor'' is common between [[Cooktown]] and [[Townsville]].<ref name="morcombe" /> The species is listed as being of [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name="buchert">{{Cite web
| last = Butchart
| first = Stuart
| author2 = Jonathan Ekstrom
| author3 = Matt Harding
| title = Species factsheet: Neochmia temporalis
| work = BirdLife
| access-date = 2010-04-22
| year = 2009
| url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=8674&m=0
| archive-date = 2007-09-29
| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20070929111317/http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=8674&m=0
}}</ref> There are no key threatening processes for ''N. temporalis'', although the Pest Animal Control CRC suggests that the introduced [[nutmeg mannikin]] (''Lonchura punctulata''), which currently threatens some native [[Munia|mannikins]] through increased competition, maybe a minor threat to ''N. lateralis'' in northern Queensland.<ref name="norris">{{Cite conference
| publisher = Pest Animal Control CRC
| last = Norris
| first = Andrew
|author2=Tim Low |author3=Iain Gordon |author4=Glen Saunders |author5=Steven Lapidge |author6=Keryn Lapidge |author7=Tony Peacock |author8=Roger Pech
 | title = Review of the management of feral animals and their impact on biodiversity in the Rangelands
| date = June 2005
| url = http://www.feral.org.au/feral_documents/Rangelands.pdf
}}{{dead link|date=March 2024}}</ref>

In regional areas, the species needs adequate shrub density to provide cover and foraging places. The species has been noted to decline or even disappear in areas that have been cleared or grazed, especially in combination with drought.<ref name="ford">{{Cite journal
| doi = 10.1016/S0006-3207(00)00101-4
| issn = 0006-3207
| volume = 97
| issue = 1
| pages = 71–88
| last1 = Ford
| first1 = Hugh A.
|author-link1=Hugh Alastair Ford
|last2=Barrett 
|first2=Geoffrey W.
|last3=Saunders
|first3=Denis A.
|author-link3=Denis A. Saunders
|last4=Recher
|first4=Harry F.
|author-link4=Harry Frederick Recher
| title = Why have birds in the woodlands of Southern Australia declined?
| journal = [[Biological_Conservation_(journal)|Biological Conservation]]
| date = January 2001
| bibcode = 2001BCons..97...71F
}}</ref>

==Biology and ecology==
The red-browed finch is highly sociable and is usually seen in small flocks of 10 to 20 individuals. Flocks are sedentary or nomadic in their local area. Flocks prefer semi-open woodland, especially edges of forests, where brushy scrub meets cleared areas, especially near creeks.

The finch makes short, piping high-pitched cheeps. When disturbed, the whole flock will disperse, cheeping, and re-congregate nearby.

''N. temporalis'' is a seed eater, living mostly on grass and sedge seed, but will happily feed on many non-native seeds. Wild birds will even enter large-mesh aviaries in suburban areas to eat seed, given the opportunity.<ref name="todd">{{Cite journal
| doi = 10.1071/MU9960245
| issn = 0158-4197
| volume = 96
| issue = 4
| pages = 245–249
| last = Todd
| first = M. K.
| title = Diet and foraging behaviour of Red-browed Finches ''Neochmia temporalis'' near Newcastle, New South Wales
| journal = Emu
| year = 1996
| bibcode = 1996EmuAO..96..245T
}}</ref>

The red-browed finch builds a large domed [[nest]] with a side entrance, woven from grass and small twigs. Nests are usually built {{Convert|2-3|m|ft}} above the ground in dense shrubs. Nesting is communal. Both parents share nest building, incubation of the eggs, and feed the young together.<ref name="birdsinbackyards">{{Cite web
| author = Birds in Backyards
| title = Red-browed Finch (Neochmia temporalis) Fact sheet
| work = BirdsinBackyards.net
| access-date = 2010-04-23
| date = 2007-02-19
| url = http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Neochmia-temporalis
| archive-date = 2010-03-16
| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100316034744/http://birdsinbackyards.net/species/Neochmia-temporalis
}}</ref> Four to six white eggs are laid per clutch two or three times per year, between October and April. Juveniles are fully independent within 28 days.<ref name="birdcare">{{Cite web
| last = BirdCare
| title = Red browed Finch
| work = BirdCare.com.au
| access-date = 2024-03-26
| date = 2008-01-29
| url = http://www.birdcare.com.au/red_browed_finch.htm
|author=<!--Not stated-->
}}</ref>

Red-browed finches are common [[aviary]] birds. The red-browed finch will sometimes hybridise with the [[star finch]], [[crimson finch]], and [[zebra finch]] if kept together in captivity.<ref name="DEWHA">{{Cite tech report
| institution = Department of the Climate Change, Energy the Environment and Water
| title = ''Neochmia ruficauda clarescens'' — Star Finch (Cape York Peninsula)
|author=<!--Not stated-->
| location = Canberra
| work = [[Species Profile and Threats Database]]
| access-date = 2024-03-26
| date = 2020
| url = http://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicspecies.pl?taxon_id=67118
}}</ref>
The species has been introduced to [[southwest Australia]], where it is sometimes confused with the red-eared firetail ''[[Stagonopleura oculata]]''.<ref name="Morcombe1986" />

zh.extract:
红眉火尾雀（学名：Neochmia temporalis），是梅花雀科星雀属的一种，为法属波利尼西亚（引进种）和澳大利亚的特有种。全球活动范围约为1,070,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
红眉火尾雀的平均体重约为11.2克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的红树林、亚热带或热带的旱林和干燥的稀树草原。


参考文献
Neochmia temporalis. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
